questions.question: Explain the difference between the 'w' permission on a file versus on a directory. (3 marks)
expectedPoints: On a file: The 'w' permission allows a user to modify, truncate, or overwrite the contents of the file.
On a directory: The 'w' permission allows a user to create, delete, or rename files within that directory.
Crucial point: Deleting a file requires write permission on the directory containing it, not on the file itself.

questions.question: A collaborative project directory '/shared/project' is owned by the group 'devteam'. Describe the steps and commands you would use to ensure that any file any team member creates in this directory is automatically writable by all other members of the 'devteam' group. (5 marks)
expectedPoints: The core problem is ensuring group collaboration, which is solved by the SetGID bit on a directory.
First, ensure the directory is owned by the 'devteam' group: `sudo chgrp devteam /shared/project`.
Set the SetGID bit on the directory so that new files inherit its group: `sudo chmod g+s /shared/project`.
Set appropriate permissions on the directory itself, e.g., `775` (rwxrwxr-x) to allow group members full access: `sudo chmod 775 /shared/project`.
Set a permissive umask (e.g., 002) for users so that new files are created with group write permission by default (e.g., 664). This is often done in a shared login shell or system-wide profile.
